headline: The Assignment
body: In 1943, photographer Ansel Adams traveled to the Manzanar War Relocation Center in California to document the lives of Japanese-Americans forcibly interned there during World War II.

title: Baseball game
caption: Japanese Americans observe an amateur baseball game in progress; one-story buildings and mountains in the background.

title: Tojo Miatake [i.e. Tōyō Miyatake] Family
caption: Tōyō Miyatake, full-length portrait, standing in his children's bedroom looking at his young daughter seated at a desk, drawing, while her mother stands behind her.

title: Roy Takeno
caption: Photo shows editor Roy Takeno reading a copy of the Manzanar Free Press in front of the newspaper office at the Manzanar War Relocation Center; with mountains in the background.

title: Manzanar Relocation Center from tower
caption: Bird's-eye view of grounds from guard tower; view west, showing buildings, roads, and Sierra Nevada mountains in background.

body: In 1944, Adams published "Born Free and Equal", a selection of his Manzanar images paired with his own text, which made the San Francisco Chronicle's bestseller list in 1945.

When Adams offered the collection to the Library of Congress in 1965, he described it as an important historical document of people who, suffering under a great injustice, built a vital community in an arid environment.

In 1988, Congress formally apologized for the "grave injustice" done to Japanese-Americans, declaring the internments were motivated by racial prejudice and wartime hysteria, and authorized a $20,000 payment to survivors.
